identity: Potassium sorbate = potassium (2E,4E)-hexa-2,4-dienoate; the potassium salt of sorbic acid (2,4-hexadienoic acid). Salt form used here: potassium salt (chosen over free sorbic acid solely because it is far more water-soluble; the active antimicrobial species is the undissociated free sorbic acid liberated at low pH). Synonyms: E202, potassium (E,E)-sorbate, 2,4-hexadienoic acid potassium salt. CAS 24634-61-5 (potassium sorbate); parent sorbic acid CAS 110-44-1. Molar mass 150.22 g/mol. In the current formula it appears as a liquid stock dosed by volume ("K-sorbate X mL / Y mL") in S2/S3/S4/S5/F5; assuming a conventional ~25% w/v stock this lands at ~3000-3300 ppm (~0.3%) in the concentrate and ~750-830 ppm after the 1:3 use-dilution (Tier 2 assumption — stock concentration is UNKNOWN from the spreadsheet label and must be confirmed). [Wikipedia/PubChem infobox Tier 2; ChemicalBook CAS 24634-61-5 Tier 2]
r1_solubility: Aqueous solubility of potassium sorbate ~58.2 g/100 mL (582 g/L) at 20C, rising only slightly to ~58.5 g/100 mL at 100C (Wikipedia/PubChem infobox, Tier 2). This is the salt; the free sorbic ACID is poorly soluble (~0.16 g/100 mL = ~1.6 g/L at 20C) — the 1.95 g/L figure that appears in some supplier pages is the free-acid value, not the salt, and was a data-collision artifact in search. Intended in-solution concentration ~3000-3300 ppm (~3.0-3.3 g/L) in the concentrate. Headroom = 582 / 3.3 = ~175x. SOLUBILITY VERDICT: OK by a very wide margin (>>3). Solubility is NOT the limiting property for potassium sorbate in this formula — it dissolves trivially. The problem is entirely R2/R3 (pH-dependent efficacy), not getting it into solution. [Wikipedia potassium sorbate infobox 58.2% @20C, Tier 2; sorbic acid free-acid solubility ~0.16 g/100mL, UCDavis Viticulture/Enology & manufaktur-pur, Tier 2]
r2_stability: EFFICACY (the load-bearing defect): the antimicrobial actor is UNDISSOCIATED sorbic acid; pKa = 4.76 (UCDavis Enology, Tier 2). By Henderson-Hasselbalch the undissociated active fraction is pH 5.8 = 8.4%, pH 6.0 = 5.4%, pH 6.2 = 3.5% (computed). At the formula pH 5.8-6.2 only ~3.5-8.4% of the dosed sorbate is in the active form; Eklund 1983 (J Appl Bacteriol 54(3):383-9, PMID 6409875, Tier 1) showed undissociated acid is 10-600x more potent than dissociated and that above pH 6 the weak dissociated form supplies >50% of (already weak) inhibition for most organisms — i.e. sorbate is operating near the top of its useful pH range and is marginal at pH 6, collapsing further toward pH 6.5 (works best <pH 6.5; foodadditives.net/Elchemy, Tier 2). CHEMICAL STABILITY: dry crystalline sorbate is stable for years; IN AQUEOUS SOLUTION it autoxidizes — >50% lost in a few months at slightly elevated temperature via oxidation/polymerization; oxidation is FASTER at low pH, with light, and with heat; major degradation products acetaldehyde and beta-carboxylacrolein, the latter a browning precursor (Sofos/Chemistry of sorbates; ResearchGate "system composition" study, Tier 2). Shelf-life of a wet, warm, light-exposed concentrate is therefore months, not years, and the protective fraction is already low. SOLE-PRESERVATIVE adequacy at formula pH 5.8-6.2: NOT adequately demonstrated for an unrefrigerated, high-sugar, amino-acid-rich, repeatedly-opened concentrate — BLOCK-severity as a stand-alone system. After 1:3 dilution (still ~pH 6) efficacy is worse. STORAGE: cool, dark, antioxidant/low-O2, acidified <pH 5.2 if wet must be retained. [PMID 6409875 Tier 1; sorbate chemistry reviews Tier 2]
r3_conflicts: Pair -> mechanism -> mitigation. (1) K-sorbate + reducing sugars/amino acids + heat (S2: Na-ascorbate, corn syrup, maltodextrin, D-ribose; S3/S4: betaine, taurine, citrulline, B-vitamins) -> sorbate autoxidation product beta-carboxylacrolein reacts with amino acids/proteins to form brown pigments; glycine specifically accelerates sorbate degradation+browning -> sorbate ADDS to the existing Maillard problem rather than just sitting inert; mitigation: remove from any reducing-sugar+amine matrix (i.e. delete the wet sugar SKUs), keep dry. (2) K-sorbate + formula pH 5.8-6.2 -> only 3.5-8.4% undissociated active, so the preservative is intrinsically near-useless at the chosen pH regardless of other ingredients -> mitigation requires dropping pH <=5.0-5.2 (raises active fraction to >=36%), which conflicts with the heavy citrate buffering already present. (3) K-sorbate + heavy Na/K-citrate buffer load -> citrate holds pH up near 6 and resists the acidification sorbate needs -> mechanism is pH-antagonism; mitigation: reduce citrate (already planned in redesign) or acidify with citric acid. (4) K-sorbate contributes K+ to the K/Na electrolyte/DEB ledger -> at ~3 g/L sorbate this is ~20 mM K added, non-trivial vs the formula's electrolyte targets -> account for it in DEB math; trivial once the wet SKUs are deleted. (5) No precipitation/chelation issue with Ca/Mg (sorbate does not chelate divalents); redox: sorbate is itself oxidation-prone and trace Fe/Cu accelerate its loss, same catalysts that hit ascorbate/NAC -> mitigation: chelator/low-metal water, or go dry. [PMID 6409875 Tier 1; sorbate browning/glycine chemistry reviews Tier 2]
avian_evidence: Potassium sorbate is a PRESERVATIVE/excipient, not a nutrient — there is no avian requirement and it is not an active to be "dosed" for the bird. Avian SAFETY/tolerance: GRAS food preservative; ADI for sorbates 0-25 mg/kg bw (JECFA/EFSA human basis, NOT to be imported as an avian limit per frame). Poultry-specific use IS documented but as ANTIMICROBIAL, not nutrition: potassium-sorbate dips extend shelf-life of poultry parts (Cunningham 1979, J Food Sci 44:863, Tier 1/2) and a potassium-sorbate dip reduced microbial load on freshly processed poultry (PMID 30795050, Tier 1); organic-acid drinking-water blends (including sorbate-class acids) reduced Campylobacter in broilers (PMC9597105, Tier 1). NO avian study establishes a maximum-tolerable oral sorbate level or any harm at food-preservative concentrations (~0.1-0.3%); at ~750-830 mg/L in a sipped drink the per-session intake for a 2.5 kg bird (~96 mL) is ~72-80 mg, well inside food-additive norms and renally/metabolically handled (sorbate is metabolized like a fatty acid to CO2/water). NO AVIAN TOXICITY DATA at these levels = no safety blocker; the issue is efficacy, not bird harm. [PMID 30795050 Tier 1; PMC9597105 Tier 1; Cunningham 1979 Tier 2]
verdict: SKIP / DELETE from the redesigned system (applies to the wet liquids S2,S3,S4,S5,F5 — all CUT in the winning dry-first design) and DO NOT add to the fight-day drink or the dry preload. Rationale: (a) at formula pH 5.8-6.2 only 3.5-8.4% is the active undissociated acid, so as a SOLE preservative it is BLOCK-severity inadequate for an unrefrigerated, sugar/amino-acid-rich concentrate (PMID 6409875); (b) in solution it autoxidizes (>50% loss in months) and its degradation product beta-carboxylacrolein drives amino-acid browning, ADDING to the formula's Maillard load; (c) the master design move removes water entirely, which makes any preservative unnecessary — the dry preload has no water activity and the fight-day ORS stick is reconstituted fresh and discarded same-day, acidified to pH ~3.8-4.2. Net: potassium sorbate is solved by deletion, not by re-dosing. CONDITIONAL fallback ONLY if a wet bottled SKU is ever forced against advice: it must be reformulated to pH <=5.0-5.2 (raising active fraction to >=36%), combined with a co-preservative (e.g. sodium benzoate, whose pKa ~4.2 gives even less margin at pH 6 — also weak here) under a validated challenge test, NOT sorbate alone. No safety/dose problem for the bird at food levels; this is purely a producibility/efficacy SKIP.
cautions: WRITTEN NOTES (non-blocking): (1) The current label doses K-sorbate as "X mL" with the stock concentration UNSTATED — the actual delivered ppm is unknowable from the spreadsheet; if the stock is weaker than the assumed 25% w/v, the wet products may have been under-preserved in practice, a real microbial-safety concern for any drink stored warm during a multi-hour fight day. (2) Sorbate at pH 6 gives a FALSE sense of preservation — handlers may believe the wet products are protected when the active fraction is only ~5%; this is the single most important welfare-relevant note because microbial growth in a warm sugar/amino-acid drink offered to the bird is the main hygiene risk the dry/make-fresh redesign specifically eliminates. (3) Sorbate is not inert filler: its oxidation products participate in browning with the formula's amino acids — leaving it in a wet sugar matrix is mildly counterproductive, not neutral. (4) No bird-toxicity concern at the intended concentrations; do not flag as harmful to the animal. (5) If vitamin C or any wet product is ever retained, do NOT rely on sorbate alone and never store reconstituted product warm.
sources: Eklund T. The antimicrobial effect of dissociated and undissociated sorbic acid at different pH levels. J Appl Bacteriol. 1983 Jun;54(3):383-9. PMID 6409875. (Tier 1 — undissociated 10-600x more potent; dissociated supplies >50% of inhibition above pH 6)
Dickens JA et al. / Antimicrobial Efficacy of a Potassium Sorbate Dip on Freshly Processed Poultry. PMID 30795050. (Tier 1 — poultry antimicrobial use)

Antimicrobial effect of a drinking-water additive comprising four organic acids on Campylobacter load in broilers. Poult Sci, PMC9597105. (Tier 1 — organic-acid drinking-water antimicrobial in broilers)
Cunningham FE. Shelf-life and quality characteristics of poultry parts dipped in potassium sorbate. J Food Sci. 1979;44:863. (Tier 2 — poultry sorbate dip)
Potassium sorbate infobox — water solubility 58.2% (g/100mL) at 20C, 58.5 g/100mL at 100C; M 150.22 g/mol; CAS 24634-61-5. Wikipedia/PubChem (Tier 2 physicochemical constant)
Sorbic acid pKa 4.76; undissociated form is the active antimicrobial; works best <pH 6.5. UC Davis Viticulture & Enology common chemical reagents (Tier 2)
Sorbic acid free-acid solubility ~0.16 g/100mL at 20C. UC Davis Enology / manufaktur-pur sorbic acid page (Tier 2)

Sofos JN, Busta FF / 'Chemistry of sorbates' reviews — aqueous autoxidation, >50% loss in months at elevated temp, acetaldehyde + beta-carboxylacrolein degradation products, beta-carboxylacrolein browns with amino acids, glycine accelerates degradation/browning; oxidation faster at low pH/light/heat. Food Reviews International 10(1) and ResearchGate 'Effect of system composition on potassium sorbate degradation' (Tier 2)
Henderson-Hasselbalch undissociated fraction at pH 5.8/6.0/6.2 = 8.4/5.4/3.5% (computed from pKa 4.76)
JECFA/EFSA sorbates ADI 0-25 mg/kg bw (human regulatory basis; per frame NOT applied as avian limit) (Tier 2)
blocking_gaps: UNKNOWN: actual concentration of the K-sorbate liquid stock used in the spreadsheet ('X mL' dosing) — the delivered ppm (and therefore whether the legacy wet products were ever adequately preserved) cannot be computed without it; assumed ~25% w/v (Tier 2 assumption).

UNKNOWN: no measured potassium-sorbate efficacy / challenge-test data for THIS specific high-sugar, high-amino-acid, pH ~6 concentrate — adequacy at pH 6 is inferred from pKa + Eklund 1983, not directly tested on this matrix.

NO AVIAN DATA: no poultry oral maximum-tolerable / NOAEL study for potassium sorbate at drinking-water concentrations (only dip/surface and organic-acid-blend studies exist); safety at intended levels is inferred, not avian-measured. Not a blocker for the SKIP verdict but a genuine evidence gap if a wet SKU were ever pursued.

corrections: Citation locator imprecision (non-load-bearing): the broiler Campylobacter drinking-water study is cited as 'PMC9597105' but that exact PMC ID could not be confirmed. The underlying real paper exists — 'Antimicrobial effect of a drinking-water additive comprising four organic acids on Campylobacter load in broilers' (Poult Sci, ScienceDirect S0032579122005053), with related PMID 24592942. Fix the locator to the correct PMID/DOI; the claim itself stands.

Minor: Cunningham 1979 is labeled Tier 2 but it is a peer-reviewed Journal of Food Science article (DOI 10.1111/j.1365-2621.1979.tb08522.x) and could be ranked Tier 1/2; the dip used was up to 10% sorbate giving ~20-day shelf-life at 4C (page detail differs slightly from the '44:863' string but the paper is genuine). Not a substantive error.

Optional precision: the '~25% w/v stock' assumption driving the ppm estimate remains UNKNOWN from the spreadsheet — the dossier already correctly flags this as a blocking gap, so no correction needed, but the derived ~750-830 ppm post-dilution figure should be presented as assumption-dependent everywhere it appears (it mostly is).
_verify.notes: Adversarial verification of all load-bearing claims passed. Eklund 1983 (PMID 6409875) is REAL and the dossier's paraphrase is exact: undissociated sorbic acid 10-600x more potent than dissociated, and dissociated supplies >50% of inhibition above pH 6 for most organisms tested. PMID 30795050 (K-sorbate dip on freshly processed poultry) and Cunningham 1979 (J Food Sci, DOI 10.1111/j.1365-2621.1979.tb08522.x) are both REAL and support the poultry-antimicrobial claims. R1 numbers verified independently: K-sorbate solubility 58.2 g/100mL @20C, free sorbic acid 0.16 g/100mL @20C, pKa 4.76 — all confirmed; the salt-vs-free-acid distinction and ~175x headroom are correct, and the dossier correctly identifies that the 1.95 g/L figure is a free-acid/data-collision artifact. R2/R3 Henderson-Hasselbalch undissociated active fractions recomputed by hand from pKa 4.76: pH 5.8=8.4%, 6.0=5.4%, 6.2=3.5%, and pH 5.0=~36.5% — all match the dossier exactly, NO inversion (lower pH correctly = MORE active undissociated form). The core thesis (sorbate is near the top of its useful pH range at formula pH 5.8-6.2 and is marginal as a sole preservative; aqueous autoxidation; beta-carboxylacrolein browning adds to Maillard load; deletion via dry-first redesign solves it) is internally consistent and well-sourced. Avian framing is correct: sorbate treated as preservative/excipient not nutrient, JECFA/EFSA ADI explicitly flagged as human-only and NOT imported as an avian limit, no fabricated avian toxicity blocker, per-session ~72-80 mg intake correctly framed as inside food-additive norms. Three honest blocking gaps (unknown stock concentration, no challenge-test on THIS matrix, no avian oral NOAEL) are appropriately disclosed and do not undermine the SKIP/DELETE verdict. Only defect found is a single citation-locator imprecision on the Campylobacter PMC ID, which does not change any conclusion. Verdict CONFIRMED.
